title: Fashioning Appetite: Restaurants and the Making of Modern Identity By Joanne Finkelstein Columbia University Press. 2014. 224 pages. $35 cloth
abstract: We are what we eat. Certainly we have been told so often enough. For Joanne Finkelstein, we are even more fundamentally where we eat. The data tell us what we can all confirm from our own experience. More and more of us more and more often eat out in the semipublic, semiprivate social spaces that we call restaurants. In a world that so easily gives itself over to food obsessions, restaurants come in every imaginable variety. As yet unimagined venues will turn up tomorrow. Pop-up restaurants, anyone? Are they “real” restaurants? It is definitely eating out. What about food trucks? The actual attributes of the public spaces where we eat outside the home do not much concern Finkelstein. Although Fashioning Appetite refers to a fair number of “real” restaurants, the references are illustrative rather than evidentiary. This paucity of the nitty-gritty of restaurant life is a function of an indifference—a principled indifference, as it turns out. The book is unconcerned with the differences between one restaurant and another, that is, all the particulars that drive word-of-mouth assessments, restaurant reviews, and blog ratings (e.g., Yelp and its spawn).
